examples.title: Basic Interrupt Handling Pseudocode
examples.description: Demonstrates the conceptual structure of interrupt service routine handling
examples.code:
// MAIN PROGRAM
PROGRAM Main
    ENABLE_INTERRUPTS()
    WHILE TRUE DO
        Execute main program tasks
    END WHILE
END PROGRAM

// INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE
PROCEDURE InterruptHandler
    SAVE_REGISTERS()        // Save CPU state
    
    READ_INTERRUPT_STATUS() // Determine interrupt source
    SWITCH interrupt_type
        CASE KEYBOARD:
            HandleKeyboard()
        CASE TIMER:
            HandleTimer()
        CASE DISK:
            HandleDiskIO()
    END SWITCH
    
    ACKNOWLEDGE_INTERRUPT() // Signal controller
    RESTORE_REGISTERS()     // Restore CPU state
    RETURN_FROM_INTERRUPT() // IRET instruction
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the typical structure of interrupt handling: saving state, determining the interrupt source, processing the interrupt, and restoring state before returning to the main program.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for ISR execution time varies by device
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for fixed register save area

examples.title: x86 Interrupt Handler in C
examples.description: Example of setting up an interrupt handler in C for x86 architecture
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates setting up an interrupt descriptor table (IDT) entry for handling hardware interrupts on x86 systems. It shows how to configure the handler address, attributes, and how to acknowledge the interrupt to the PIC.

examples.title: Simulated Interrupt Controller
examples.description: Python simulation of interrupt priority handling and vector generation
examples.code:
class InterruptController:
    """Simulates a programmable interrupt controller"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        # Interrupt request register (pending requests)
        self.irq_request = 0
        # Interrupt service register (being serviced)
        self.irq_service = 0
        # Interrupt mask register (enabled interrupts)
        self.irq_mask = 0xFFFF  # All enabled by default
        # Priority table (lower number = higher priority)
        self.priority = {
            0: 0,   # IRQ0 - Timer (highest)
            1: 1,   # IRQ1 - Keyboard
            2: 2,   # IRQ2 - Cascade
            3: 3,   # IRQ3
            4: 4,   # IRQ4
            5: 5,   # IRQ5
            6: 6,   # IRQ6 - Floppy
            7: 7,   # IRQ7
        }
        self.vector_base = 32  # IRQ0 maps to vector 32
    
    def raise_interrupt(self, irq_line):
        """Device raises an interrupt request"""
        if not (self.irq_mask & (1 << irq_line)):
            # Interrupt is masked, ignore
            return False
        self.irq_request |= (1 << irq_line)
        return True
    
    def get_highest_priority_irq(self):
        """Find the highest priority pending interrupt"""
        pending = self.irq_request & ~self.irq_service & self.irq_mask
        
        if pending == 0:
            return None
        
        # Find lowest numbered IRQ (highest priority)
        for irq in range(8):
            if pending & (1 << irq):
                return irq
        return None
    
    def acknowledge_interrupt(self):
        """CPU acknowledges interrupt, returns vector"""
        irq = self.get_highest_priority_irq()
        
        if irq is None:
            return None
        
        # Mark as being serviced
        self.irq_service |= (1 << irq)
        
        # Return interrupt vector
        return self.vector_base + irq
    
    def end_of_interrupt(self, irq_line):
        """ISR signals end of interrupt processing"""
        self.irq_service &= ~(1 << irq_line)
        self.irq_request &= ~(1 << irq_line)
    
    def mask_interrupt(self, irq_line):
        """Disable a specific interrupt"""
        self.irq_mask &= ~(1 << irq_line)
    
    def unmask_interrupt(self, irq_line):
        """Enable a specific interrupt"""
        self.irq_mask |= (1 << irq_line)


# Example usage simulation
def simulate_interrupt_handling():
    pic = InterruptController()
    
    # Keyboard (IRQ1) raises interrupt
    print("Keyboard raises interrupt...")
    pic.raise_interrupt(1)
    
    # CPU acknowledges interrupt
    vector = pic.acknowledge_interrupt()
    print(f"CPU got interrupt vector: {vector}")
    
    # ISR processes interrupt
    print("Keyboard ISR executing...")
    
    # ISR completes
    pic.end_of_interrupt(1)
    print("Interrupt cleared")

# Run simulation
simulate_interrupt_handling()
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates a programmable interrupt controller (PIC) handling multiple interrupt requests, priority resolution, masking, and vector generation. It demonstrates the key concepts of interrupt management in software.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of IRQ lines for priority resolution
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) fixed overhead plus O(n) for interrupt state

examples.title: Polled vs Interrupt-Based I/O Comparison
examples.description: Algorithm comparing CPU utilization between polling and interrupt-driven I/O
examples.explanation: This comparison demonstrates why interrupt-driven I/O is more efficient than polling. With polling, the CPU wastes cycles continuously checking device status. With interrupts, the CPU can perform useful work while waiting for the device.
examples.timeComplexity: Polling: O(1) per check but many iterations; Interrupt: O(1) per transfer
examples.spaceComplexity: Polling: O(1); Interrupt: O(buffer_size) for data transfer
